title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Sorafenib
content: It inhibits multiple receptor tyrosine kinases, including VEGFR 1, 2, 3, PDGFR beta, c-kit, FMS-like tyrosine kinase 3, rearranged upon transfection, and rapidly accelerated fibrosarcoma kinase. While c-kit, FLT Kinase 3, and RET are located upon the cell surface, Raf is an intracellular enzyme (common to all receptor tyrosine kinase pathways). REA disruption of the signal transduction, with inhibition of transcription of various proteins, is also observed due to inhibition of the intracellular phosphorylation pathways, including the Raf/MEF/ERK and the PI3K/m TOR/AKT pathways. The anti-angiogenic effect of sorafenib is attributed to its effect on angiogenesis and lymphangiogenesis, which is mediated via the VEGFR 1,2 (angiogenesis) and 3 (lymphangiogenesis), respectively. Inhibition of the Platelet-derived growth factor receptor on the surface of the pericytes and smooth muscle cells also contributes to this effect. Sorafenib is administered orally at 400 mg twice daily, with an oral bioavailability of 92 percent. More than 9.5 percent of the drug is protein-bound to albumin and alpha 1 acid glycoprotein. It is mainly metabolized to an N oxide metabolite by the Cytochrome p 450 enzyme system. The elimination half-life of the molecule is between 20 to 39 hours. Excretion of the glucuronide metabolite occurs via the urinary and fecal routes. ESMO magnitude of clinical benefit scale ranking of 4. [98] [99]

title: Drug Absorption -- Issues of Concern
content: With active diffusion, the carrier binds to form a complex with the drug. This complex facilitates the transportation of the drug across the membrane and then disassociates on the other side. The carrier molecule may be highly specific to the drug molecule. Drugs sharing similar structures can compete for the carrier in absorption sites. Since only a small number of carrier molecules are available, the binding sites on the carrier may become saturated if the drug concentration is very high, after which the dose increases do not affect the drug concentration. While some transporters facilitate absorption, transporters such as P-glycoprotein (P-gp) can effectively impede drug absorption. P-gp (MDR1) is an energy-dependent efflux transporter that facilitates the secretion of molecules back into the intestinal lumen, thereby restricting overall absorption. [5] Facilitated diffusion is another transporter system that plays a minor role in drug absorption. It is similar to the active diffusion system in that both are saturable and exhibit drug selectivity and competition kinetics. However, the main differences are that facilitated diffusion does not require energy and, unlike active transport, does not enable the movement against a concentration gradient. An example of a facilitated diffusion system is the organic cation transporter 1 (OCT1), which facilitates the movement of drugs like metformin. [6]

title: Pharmacogenomics Overview -- Clinical Significance -- Drug Transporters and Genes
content: ABCB1 is also involved in drug-drug interactions. Some known ABCB1 inhibitors include verapamil, diltiazem, and cyclosporine, as they can increase the bioavailability of ABCB1 substrates. In contrast, inducers, such as rifampin, can decrease its bioavailability. The ABC transporters are associated with drug resistance; for example, ABCB1 decreases intracellular drug concentration, thereby reducing drug efficacy. The BCRP (breast cancer resistance protein), also known as ABCG2, is an efflux pump that transports the anticancer drugs methotrexate and topotecan. Encoded by the ABCG2 gene, ABCG2 is found in the liver, kidney, central nervous system, and gastrointestinal tract. [39] Overexpression of ABCG2 in cancer cells leads to multidrug resistance. A straightforward explanation is that drug absorption is limited in the ABCG2-overexpressing intestinal cells. Other evidence suggests that enhanced ABCG2 activity is involved in the epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition process, which is associated with cancer progression. [46]

title: Rivaroxaban -- Contraindications
content: Drug-drug interactions: Potentially significant interactions may exist which require dose or frequency adjustment, additional monitoring, and/or consideration of alternative therapy. Consult a drug-interaction database for more detailed information. DOACs like rivaroxaban interact with potent CYP3A inhibitors and P-glycoprotein (P-GP) inhibitors, and breast cancer resistance protein Bcrp (ABCG2) inhibitors (mainly comprising azole-antimycotics, apart from fluconazole and HIV protease inhibitors), which are multi-pathway inhibitors of rivaroxaban clearance and elimination. [15] Its use should also be avoided when using other anticoagulants like unfractionated heparin, low molecular weight heparins (e.g., enoxaparin), fondaparinux, warfarin, apixaban, or dabigatran, except when switching therapy to or from rivaroxaban. Rivaroxaban should be combined with simultaneous use with ASA plus clopidogrel (or other thienopyridines, such as ticlopidine or prasugrel) or plus another adenosine diphosphate (ADP) P2Y12 receptor antagonists (such as ticagrelor) should be avoided. [11]

title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Contraindications
content: Co-administration of ubrogepant with a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor is contraindicated. If the patient is on moderate CYP3A4 inhibitors such as ciprofloxacin, cyclosporine, and grapefruit juice, an initial dose of 50mg may be taken. But avoid the use of the second 50 mg dose within 24 hours. A loss of ubrogepant exposure, and thus efficacy, is expected in patients taking strong CYP3A4 inducers such as rifampin, barbiturates, and phenytoin. So, avoid concomitant use. If ubrogepant and moderate or weak CYP3A4 inducers are given concomitantly, dose adjustment is recommended. Ubrogepant acts as a substrate of P-gp and BCRP efflux transporters, but clinical drug interaction studies with inhibitors of only P-gp and/or BCRP (such as carvedilol and quinidine) were not conducted. Therefore, dose adjustment is recommended when administering these concomitantly. Avoid the use of ubrogepant in end-stage renal disease (CLcr <15 mL/min). [16]

title: Pharmacogenomics Overview -- Clinical Significance -- Drug Transporters and Genes
content: Drug transporters are involved in drug absorption, distribution, and elimination, as they determine a drug's fate in the body. These transporters are proteins that regulate the transportation of drugs by facilitating or restricting their movement across cellular membranes. The genes and variants of these transporters, subject to the nature of polymorphism, may exhibit different transport behaviors and result in varied outcomes. Drug-transporting systems are classified into 2 major families as follows: ATP-binding cassette (ABC) transporters are active efflux mechanisms that utilize ATP to extrude drugs against concentration gradients. Solute carrier (SLC) transporters facilitate passive or secondary active transport of drugs across cellular membranes. [41]

title: Pharmacogenomics Overview -- Clinical Significance -- Drug Transporters and Genes
content: OATP1B1, encoded by SLCO1B1, is specifically expressed on the basolateral membrane of hepatocytes and plays an indispensable role in the hepatic uptake and elimination of negatively charged (or amphipathic) organic compounds. Substrates of OATP1B1 include bile salts, steroid hormones, statins, bilirubin, thyroid hormones, prostaglandins, methotrexate, irinotecan, repaglinide, and some antiviral and anticancer drugs. Statins are known to be subject to SLCO1B1 polymorphism. Drug interactions with statins occur every day during combination therapy, according to numerous published studies. For instance, when statins are used in combination with cyclosporine, rifampin, and gemfibrozil, excessive exposure to statins may occur, potentially leading to rhabdomyolysis and life-threatening consequences due to drug interactions inhibiting the function of OATP1B1. SLCO1B1 (OATP1B1) includes the c.521T>C variant that reduces statin uptake, thereby increasing the risk of statin-induced myopathy. SLC22A1 (OCT1) variants can influence the efficacy of metformin by altering its hepatic uptake.

title: Hepatobiliary Tract Cancer(Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- Positive Margins
content: No single or combined therapy in unresectable, locally advanced, and metastatic cholangiocarcinoma will achieve a cure; rather the focus is on palliative intervention and extension of survival. All patients with advanced and unresectable disease should be offered a clinical trial when feasible. Patients with good performance status (ECOG 0-1) for chemotherapy could consider first-line option Gem [1000 mg/m2] plus Cisplatin (Cis) [25 mg/m2] on days one and eight every three weeks with acceptable toxicity in low dose cis as published in ABC-02 randomized phase 2 study which included 242 metastatic bile duct cancer patients and showed a mOS of 11.7 months in the combination arm when compared to 8.3 months of Gem alone (HR, 0.70; p0.002). Other first-line, gem-based combinations are not randomized studies and have reported a mOS for GemCap 12.7 months and Gem with Oxaliplatin 14.3 months both on a selected population of patients. Alternative 5-FU-based combination plus Cis 11.5 months or Cap 11.3 months. Patients with poor performance may receive oral Cap which reported a mOS of 8.1 months when compared to the best supportive care of 4.5 months. Future clinical trials with targeted therapy, including FGFR, MEK, and other drugs tailored per the tumor molecular profile, are ongoing and preliminary reports are promising. [14] [15]

title: Pharmacogenomics Overview -- Introduction
content: Over the years, pharmacogenomics has developed several tributaries. The hepatic metabolism of drugs is catalyzed by enzymes, including the cytochrome P450 (CYP) system, whose polymorphism has expanded to several dozen CYP genotypes. [6] Studies of these phenotypes, their involvement in drug metabolism, and their vulnerability to exogenous inhibition and induction have always made the headlines. [7] On the other hand, ATP-binding cassette transporters, such as P-glycoproteins (P-gp), facilitate the movement of exogenous agents across the cell membrane. The presence of ABC gene polymorphisms and altered P-gp expression is essential for drug response and interaction. [8] A major advancement in pharmacogenomics is the approval of histology-agnostic therapies by the United States Food and Drug Administration, in which drugs are indicated based on the presence of specific genetic alterations rather than the tumor's tissue of origin. These approvals permit the use of targeted therapies across multiple cancer types when tumors harbor the relevant companion diagnostic mutation.[ National Cancer Institute. Agnostic Cancer Therapies (PDQ®)–Health Professional Version ]

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1116
content: Cytochrome P450 3A4 inducers affect the pharmacokinetics of several drugs, including the benzodiazepine klobazam, by altering its metabolic pathway through their mechanism of action. These inducers also have a contraindicated mechanism of action with flibanserin, meaning that co‑administration can diminish flibanserin’s therapeutic effect and should be avoided. Chemically, flibanserin is derived from a benzimidazoly scaffold, linking its structure to this heterocyclic core. In addition to its metabolic considerations, flibanserin acts as a P‑glycoprotein inhibitor, influencing drug transport and potentially increasing the exposure of other substrates of this efflux pump. Recognizing the interplay among Cytochrome P450 3A4 inducers, klobazam, flibanserin, benzimidazoly chemistry, and P‑glycoprotein inhibition is essential for optimizing drug therapy and preventing adverse interactions.

title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 351
content: Potassium‑channel blockers constitute a broad pharmacological group that includes the subclass of membrane‑transport modulators; these modulators further encompass agents such as FMRFamid, which is specifically classified as a modulator of membrane transport. Within the hierarchy of molecular‑mechanism pharmacology, membrane‑transport modulators are positioned under the broader category of “pharmacological effects – molecular mechanisms,” and this category also contains the subclass of enzyme inhibitors. Enzyme inhibitors themselves are a type of protein‑synthesis inhibitor, indicating that drugs that act by inhibiting enzymes ultimately interfere with the synthesis of proteins. The dermatological drug class comprises several distinct subgroups. Keratolytics are a child category of dermatological agents, while specific drugs such as fluticasone and furocoumarins are each identified as individual members of the dermatological class. Methotrexate is also a dermatological agent; in addition to its dermatologic use, it is classified both as a non‑steroidal abortive and as an enzyme inhibitor, reflecting its multiple mechanisms of action. Because enzyme inhibitors are protein‑synthesis inhibitors, methotrexate’s inhibitory activity aligns with this broader pharmacological pathway. Cyclosporine is another dermatological agent that shares the enzyme‑inhibitor characteristic; it is linked to the enzyme‑inhibitor subclass through an inverse‑ISA relationship, indicating that cyclosporine functions as an enzyme inhibitor. Importantly, cyclosporine has a documented contraindication with the lipid‑lowering drug simvastatin, highlighting a clinically relevant drug‑interaction risk that must be considered in patient management.

title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 1067
content: Blokátory draslíkových kanálů (potassium‑channel blockers) and blokátory sodíkových kanálů (sodium‑channel blockers) are both classified as modulátory membránového transportu (membrane‑transport modulators), indicating that they act by influencing ion channel function. Within the sodium‑channel blocker group, amilorid is identified as a specific member, and this class is further nested under the broader triamteren category. Amilorid, as well as its hydrochloride form, belong to the group “Other potassium‑sparing agents in ATC,” which is itself a subclass of potassium‑sparing diuretics, highlighting amilorid’s dual role as both a sodium‑channel blocker and a potassium‑sparing diuretic. Both amilorid and conivaptan produce the physiologic effect of increased diuresis, reflecting their utility in promoting urine output. However, conivaptan carries a contraindication for patients at risk of انقطاع البول (urinary retention), a restriction that is also noted for bisoprolol, underscoring the need to avoid these agents in individuals prone to urinary outflow obstruction. Amilorid (including amilorid hydrochloride) is reported to may_treat تليف كيسي (cystic fibrosis), suggesting a therapeutic application beyond its diuretic properties. FMRFamid is another agent classified as a modulátor membránového transportu, reinforcing the concept that various pharmacologic classes—such as potassium‑channel blockers, sodium‑channel blockers, and other membrane‑transport modulators—share a common mechanism of modulating ion transport across cellular membranes. Overall, the interrelationships among these agents illustrate how specific drugs can belong to multiple hierarchical categories (e.g., amilorid as a sodium‑channel blocker, a potassium‑sparing diuretic, and a membrane‑transport modulator) and how their physiological effects, therapeutic indications, and contraindications must be considered collectively in patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
content: Potassium‑channel blockers and sodium‑channel blockers are both classified as modulators of membrane transport, reflecting their shared mechanism of altering transmembrane ion movement. Among sodium‑channel blockers, amiloride is listed under “Other potassium‑sparing agents in ATC” and functions as a potassium‑sparing diuretic that increases diuresis and may be used to treat cystic fibrosis; it is combined with the loop diuretic bumetanide in the oral tablet amiloride hydrochloride 5 mg / bumetanide 1 mg, a formulation that exploits the synergistic diuretic effect of a sodium‑channel blocker and a saluretic. Conivaptan also produces increased diuresis and, together with bisoprolol, carries a contraindication for patients with urinary retention (انقطاع البول). Aldosterone antagonists constitute a subclass of potassium‑sparing diuretics within the broader kanrenon (saluretic) class; this group includes potassium canrenate, finerenone and eplerenone, all of which block aldosterone‑mediated sodium retention while preserving potassium. Bumetanide is identified as a saluretic and is placed under the “TYPES AND MISCELLANEOUS DIURETICS” hierarchy, which also contains mercurial diuretics; both mercurial diuretics and xanthine‑derived low‑ceiling diuretics are classified as low‑ceiling diuretics excl. thiazides, providing modest diuretic effects. The “Other potassium‑sparing agents in ATC” and sodium‑channel blockers are further linked through the broader triamteren pharmacologic category. FMRFamid is explicitly categorized as a membrane‑transport modulator, reinforcing the overarching theme that diverse agents—whether targeting potassium channels, sodium channels, or aldosterone receptors—share a common mechanistic focus on modulating transmembrane ion transport. These hierarchical classifications, therapeutic roles, and safety considerations together form a coherent framework for rational pharmacotherapy and informed patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 744
content: Potassium‑channel blockers are classified as a type of membrane‑transport modulator, as are sodium‑channel blockers, which together with other agents modulate the movement of ions across cellular membranes. The drug FMRFamid belongs to this broader group of membrane‑transport modulators. Within the category of potassium‑sparing diuretics, the ATC subgroup “Other potassium‑sparing agents” includes amiloride, and this subgroup is itself a subclass of potassium‑sparing diuretics. Amiloride, which also functions as a sodium‑channel blocker by inhibiting the epithelial sodium channel, produces the physiologic effect of increased diuresis. Triamterene, another potassium‑sparing diuretic, similarly acts as a sodium‑channel blocker and is a member of the same ATC subgroup. Both amiloride and triamterene exemplify how sodium‑channel blockade contributes to potassium‑sparing diuretic activity. Conivaptan, a vasopressin antagonist, also induces increased diuresis, but its use is contraindicated in patients with urinary retention (انقطاع البول). Likewise, the β‑blocker bisoprolol is contraindicated in the presence of urinary retention, highlighting a shared safety consideration for drugs that affect fluid balance or urinary output.

title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2005
content: The “analogy a deriváty” classification groups a diverse set of pharmaceutical agents that are considered structural or functional analogues or derivatives, encompassing analgesics (aspirin, paracetamol), antiviral protease inhibitors (saquinavir), anticancer drugs (paclitaxel, bleomycin, amphotericin B), hormonal compounds (methyltestosterone), lipid‑lowering agents (orlistat), calcium‑channel blockers (diltiazem), immunosuppressants (imatinib mesylate), quinolone antibiotics (cinoxacin), anticoagulants (dikumarol), neuromuscular blockers (atrakurium), bile‑acid sequestrants (cholestyramine resin), vitamins (biotin), and enzyme replacement therapy (alglucerase, which is also linked to the broader “structural_class_of” category). A substantial subset of these agents—aspirin, alglucerase, amantadin, amphotericin B, atrakurium, biotin, cholestyramine resin, cinoxacin, dikumarol, diltiazem, imatinib mesylate, methyltestosterone, orlistat, paclitaxel, and saquinavir—is specifically contraindicated in patients with drug hypersensitivity (فرط الحساسية الدوائية). In individuals with known hypersensitivity reactions, clinicians should avoid prescribing any of these drugs to prevent immune‑mediated adverse events and should select alternative therapies that are safe for this population.
